AI capable of clinical reasoning has the potential to increase access to expertise while giving physicians more time with patients. Realizing this requires a safety-centric, evidence-based approach that moves beyond simulation into real-world clinical environments.

In

Key results from the study:  Conversational safety: No safety-related stops were required by physician supervisors across all patient interactions.  Clinical reasoning: AMIE matched final diagnoses within its top 7 possibilities in 90% of cases.  Trust: Patient attitudes

This marks a vital step in our multi-year research progression—moving from simulated clinical exams (OSCEs) to actual virtual care workflows. It provides the empirical foundation for our next phase: a nationwide randomized study with Included Health.

A year ago, we launched the Responses API.

When models can use tools, responses turn into actions.

Developers are building applications that can:
• Call tools
• Search the web and files
• Use computers
• Run multi-step workflows

We’re still so early.

Technical lessons from building computer access for agents

Making long-running workflows practical required tightening the execution loop, providing rich context via file systems, and enabling network access with security guardrails.

Here's how we equipped the Responses API

The recipe behind today’s frontier reasoning models is surprisingly similar to AlphaGo:

1) Imitate large amounts of human data

2) Scale inference compute to reason better (back then it was Monte Carlo Tree Search, today it's Chain of Thought)

3) Use RL to go beyond imitation
I believe that one day we could, but not clear if we ever should.
First we do something AlphaGo-like then maybe AlphaZero-like but that will likely be post-AGI imo, and as Noam says, we should be very careful with a step like that.

A big determinant of AI's job impact is driven by the lack of compute, especially for agentic work, which takes a lot of it. That makes AI expensive.

So companies will only want to burn compute on high-value tasks (eg coding), because, in other jobs, humans remain much cheaper.

Yes, this is the competitive advantage argument economists have been discussing for awhile, but it is coming true.

There will not be enough compute for many years to automate many human jobs, even assuming AI can do that work and companies are willing to replace the people.

It is not uncommon for engineers who are really using AI to spend thousands of dollars a day on AI. That is not an expense you are going to be willing to take for many jobs out there. Prices will drop, but demand is still going up and supply  is constrained.

Under the hood, Umberto uses the CamemBERT architecture (think BERT but for Italian) and was trained on Common Crawl data. This means it learned from massive amounts of Italian web text, giving it deep understanding of how Italian works in real contexts.

Perplexity just turned their API into a full-stack platform for building AI agents.

One API key. Four services. No more stitching together 3 different providers.

Here's what they shipped and why it matters for builders

The Sandbox API is the sleeper feature here. Code execution as a tool inside the Agent API means agents can write and run code as part of their reasoning loop.

That's the difference between an agent that suggests solutions and one that actually validates them.

What makes this compelling: most teams building agents today are managing separate providers for inference, search, embeddings, and execution. Each with its own auth, rate limits, and failure modes.

Consolidating that stack under one platform with one API key is genuinely

But how do we know if the way we evaluate them is actually meaningful?

Announcing our new paper: “Deep Research, Shallow Evaluation: A Case Study in

The bigger picture?

To build genuinely useful research agents, we need more than shallow evaluations. There's no one-size-fits-all standard for "good" research. Future evaluation frameworks need to model the diversity of user expectations, not just optimize for a single

Our recommendations:
 Use pairwise preference for system-level analysis only
 Design metric-specific annotations for metric-level insights
 Match annotator expertise to evaluation goals
 Inspect disagreements, not just agreement scores
